message.content:
You are testing ONE hypothesis for defect D65 (wanbond WAN-bonding tunnel throughput investigation). Return the structured JSON evidence block per your role contract. READ-ONLY.

**hypothesisId:** H4

**statement (verbatim):** H-A: No MSS clamping on wanbond0 — inner MTU 1400 (minus FEC parity penalty) not enforced on TCP, so full-size segments fragment over the underlay or hit a PMTUD black-hole, causing per-segment loss + retransmits

**branchContext:** Defect D65: tunnel single-flow TCP plateaus ~3.67 Mbps, cwnd stuck ~30KB, 13 retransmits/10s (loss/reorder-limited); UDP 8Mbps offered → 6.9 delivered at 13% loss with ~1s loaded RTT (idle 40ms). Pi4 edge, Starlink ~40ms RTT, o3 concentrator. This is one of 5 disjoint root hypotheses. Confirm or rule out that the wanbond0 datapath fails to clamp inner TCP MSS to the effective inner MTU (InnerMTU 1400 minus FECParityMTUPenalty), so inner TCP emits oversize segments that fragment over the underlay or black-hole PMTUD — which would directly produce loss-limited TCP. Determine: (1) what the effective inner MTU is and whether FEC further reduces it; (2) whether ANY MSS clamp is installed (in-code, iptables, or documented only as an operator step); (3) whether the tunnel fragments or silently drops oversize inner packets. Report SUPPORTS and CONTRADICTS evidence.

**leads:**
- internal/bind/mtu.go (InnerMTU, FECParityMTUPenalty, DefaultPathMTU=1500)
- docs/p1-mtu.md (MSS-clamping guidance)
- internal/bind/classify.go
- cmd/ and internal/device (wanbond0 link setup, TUN MTU)
- grep for mss/clamp/TCPMSS across repo, packaging/, and wanbond.example.toml

Emit the ### Session summary block then the fenced json block as the last content.

content.content:
1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
5		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
6	)
7	
8	// MTU accounting for the bonded datapath.
9	//
10	// A tunnelled application packet is wrapped in four nested layers before it hits
11	// the wire, each adding fixed overhead:
12	//
13	//		[ IP | UDP | outer DATA frame | WG transport | inner IP payload ]
14	//		  \___ IPv4UDPOverhead ___/   \_DataOverhead_/\_WGTransportOverhead_/
15	//
16	//	  - IP + UDP: the underlay carrying our outer datagram.
17	//	  - outer DATA frame: the bonding codec's nonce + DATA header (frame.DataOverhead).
18	//	  - WG transport: WireGuard's own data-message header + Poly1305 tag.
19	//
20	// InnerMTU sizes the TUN so a full-MTU inner packet, once wrapped in all four
21	// layers, still fits the path MTU without IP fragmentation. Fragmentation must be
22	// avoided: a fragment lost on a lossy WAN drops the whole datagram, and PMTUD
23	// black holes (ICMP "fragmentation needed" filtered by a middlebox) silently stall
24	// the tunnel. See docs/p1-mtu.md for the derivation and the MSS-clamping guidance
25	// that keeps TCP inside this budget.
26	const (
27		// DefaultPathMTU is the assumed underlay path MTU when none is measured. 1500
28		// is the near-universal Ethernet/most-broadband figure; deployments on links
29		// with a smaller MTU (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks) must lower it.
30		DefaultPathMTU = 1500
31	
32		// IPv4UDPOverhead is the outer IPv4 (20) + UDP (8) header cost. IPv4 is the
33		// conservative default here; an IPv6 underlay costs 20 bytes more (see
34		// IPv6UDPOverhead) so sizing for IPv4 and running over IPv6 only wastes a few
35		// bytes, never fragments.
36		IPv4UDPOverhead = 20 + 8
37	
38		// IPv6UDPOverhead is the outer IPv6 (40) + UDP (8) header cost.
39		IPv6UDPOverhead = 40 + 8
40	
41		// WGTransportOverhead is WireGuard's per-datagram data-message overhead: the
42		// 16-byte transport header (msg type + reserved + receiver index + counter)
43		// plus the 16-byte Poly1305 authentication tag. Amnezia junk PREFIXES add
44		// further variable bytes on top; they are handled as best-effort headroom, not
45		// subtracted here, because they are configurable and per-packet variable.
46		WGTransportOverhead = 16 + 16
47	)
48	
49	// FECParityMTUPenalty is the extra bytes a full-size FEC PARITY frame occupies on the
50	// wire over a DATA frame carrying the same-size inner payload (T24). A parity frame is
51	// nonce + PARITY header + shard payload, where the shard payload is the codec's length
52	// prefix + the FEC outer-seq prefix + the largest coded inner payload; a data frame is
53	// nonce + DATA header + that same inner payload. So the delta is:
54	//
55	//	(ParityOverhead + ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen) - DataOverhead  ==  5 bytes.
56	//
57	// When FEC is enabled the inner MTU is reduced by this penalty so a full-MTU DATA frame
58	// AND its group's PARITY frame both fit the path MTU — otherwise parity exceeds the
59	// path MTU by exactly this much and IP-fragments or is EMSGSIZE/PMTUD-blackholed, which
60	// silently kills the very redundancy FEC exists to provide on bulk full-size traffic.
61	const FECParityMTUPenalty = frame.ParityOverhead + fec.ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen - frame.DataOverhead
62	
63	// InnerMTU returns the largest inner (TUN) MTU that avoids IP fragmentation over
64	// an IPv4 underlay of the given path MTU: pathMTU minus the IP+UDP, outer DATA
65	// frame, and WireGuard transport overheads. fecEnabled additionally reserves the
66	// parity-over-data delta so a full-size parity frame also fits (see
67	// FECParityMTUPenalty); pass false when FEC is off to keep the pre-T24 budget.
68	func InnerMTU(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
69		return pathMTU - IPv4UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
70	}
71	
72	// InnerMTU6 is InnerMTU for an IPv6 underlay.
73	func InnerMTU6(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
74		return pathMTU - IPv6UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
75	}
76	
77	// fecPenalty is the inner-MTU reduction to reserve for FEC parity, or 0 when FEC is
78	// off.
79	func fecPenalty(fecEnabled bool) int {
80		if fecEnabled {
81			return FECParityMTUPenalty
82		}
83		return 0
84	}
85

1	# P1 MTU accounting and MSS-clamping guidance (T12)
2	
3	The multipath Bind (`internal/bind`, T12) wraps every opaque WireGuard datagram
4	in an outer DATA frame before it leaves a per-path socket. That outer frame is
5	pure overhead the P0 pass-through Bind did not add, so the tunnel MTU must shrink
6	to keep a full-size inner packet from fragmenting on the wire. This document
7	records the arithmetic (the source of truth is `internal/bind/mtu.go`, pinned by
8	`TestInnerMTUFixture`) and the MSS-clamping the operator must apply so TCP stays
9	inside the budget.
10	
11	## Why fragmentation must be avoided
12	
13	- **Loss amplification.** An IP-fragmented outer datagram is only delivered if
14	  *every* fragment arrives. On a lossy WAN (Starlink/5G — the target links) the
15	  probability that all fragments survive is strictly worse than for a single
16	  datagram, so fragmentation inflates the effective loss rate exactly where we
17	  can least afford it.
18	- **PMTUD black holes.** Path-MTU discovery relies on ICMP "fragmentation
19	  needed" (IPv4 DF) / "packet too big" (IPv6). Consumer CPE and carrier NAT
20	  routinely filter that ICMP, so an oversized packet is silently dropped and the
21	  sender never learns why — the tunnel stalls for large flows while small packets
22	  pass. Sizing the inner MTU conservatively side-steps PMTUD entirely.
23	
24	## The overhead stack
25	
26	A tunnelled inner packet is wrapped in four nested layers before the wire:
27	
28	```
29	[ outer IP | outer UDP | outer DATA frame | WG transport | inner IP payload ]
30	  \_______ 28 (IPv4) _______/ \___ 39 ___/ \____ 32 ____/ \___ inner MTU ___/
31	```
32	
33	| Layer | Bytes | Constant |
34	| ----- | ----- | -------- |
35	| Outer IPv4 header | 20 | `IPv4UDPOverhead` (with UDP) |
36	| Outer UDP header | 8 | " |
37	| Outer DATA frame | **39** | `frame.DataOverhead` |
38	| WireGuard transport | **32** | `WGTransportOverhead` |
39	
40	The DATA-frame overhead of **39 bytes** decomposes as: XChaCha20 nonce (24) +
41	kind discriminant (1) + outer-seq (8) + path-id (1) + fec-group (4) + flags (1).
42	DATA frames are unauthenticated (the inner WireGuard layer authenticates the real
43	payload), so they carry **no** MAC tag — the 39-byte figure is exact.
44	`TestDataOverheadMatchesEncoding` pins it to the real encoded length so the MTU
45	budget can never silently drift from the codec.
46	
47	The WireGuard transport overhead of **32 bytes** is the 16-byte data-message
48	header (message type + reserved + receiver index + counter) plus the 16-byte
49	Poly1305 tag.
50	
51	> Amnezia junk **prefixes** add further *variable* bytes on top of a real
52	> transport packet when obfuscation is configured. They are not subtracted here
53	> (they are per-packet variable and configurable); an amnezia deployment must
54	> lower the path MTU it feeds `InnerMTU` by its worst-case junk-prefix size, or
55	> accept that the largest junked packets may fragment. Wiring amnezia end-to-end
56	> is T19; this note is revisited there.
57	
58	## Computed inner MTU
59	
60	For the default 1500-byte IPv4 path MTU:
61	
62	```
63	inner MTU = 1500 − 28 (IP+UDP) − 39 (DATA frame) − 32 (WG) = 1401 bytes
64	```
65	
66	`internal/bind.InnerMTU(1500) == 1401`, asserted by `TestInnerMTUFixture`, and
67	`internal/device` sizes the TUN to exactly this value. An IPv6 underlay costs 20
68	more header bytes → `InnerMTU6(1500) == 1381`. Deployments whose real path MTU is
69	below 1500 (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks — PPPoE is 1492, and CGNAT/telco tunnels are
70	often lower) must lower the path MTU passed to `InnerMTU`; when paths differ, size
71	the tunnel to the **smallest** path's inner MTU, since the single virtual endpoint
72	carries one MTU for the whole bond.
73	
74	## MSS clamping (the operator action)
75	
76	Setting the TUN MTU bounds what the *local* stack originates, but TCP endpoints
77	*behind* the tunnel (a LAN host routed through the edge, or the concentrator's
78	downstream) negotiate their MSS from their own link MTU and will happily send
79	1460-byte segments that no longer fit once wrapped. The fix is to clamp the TCP
80	MSS of forwarded SYNs to the tunnel's inner MTU minus the inner IP+TCP headers:
81	
82	```
83	MSS = inner MTU − 40 (IPv4: 20 IP + 20 TCP) = 1401 − 40 = 1361 bytes
84	      inner MTU − 60 (IPv6: 40 IP + 20 TCP) = 1381 − 60 = 1321 bytes
85	```
86	
87	Clamp on the forwarding node (edge and concentrator) with the standard
88	`TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu` target on the tunnel interface, or an explicit value:
89	
90	```sh
91	# Clamp to the discovered PMTU (preferred — tracks the tunnel MTU automatically):
92	iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
93	          -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu
94	ip6tables -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
95	          -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu
96	
97	# Or pin an explicit MSS if a fixed lower path MTU is known:
98	iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
99	          -j TCPMSS --set-mss 1361
100	```
101	
102	`--clamp-mss-to-pmtu` is preferred: it derives the MSS from the tunnel interface
103	MTU, so it stays correct if the inner MTU is retuned (e.g. amnezia enabled, or a
104	lower real path MTU). UDP and other non-TCP traffic cannot be MSS-clamped; those
105	flows must rely on the inner MTU and, for locally originated traffic, on the
106	sender honouring the TUN MTU.
107	
108	## What T12 verifies
109	
110	- `TestInnerMTUFixture` / `TestDataOverheadMatchesEncoding` — the arithmetic and
111	  its coupling to the codec.
112	- The e2e `TestMultipathNoFragmentation` fixture sends a max-inner-MTU payload
113	  with DF set and asserts, from a packet capture on the edge egress, that no
114	  outer datagram is IP-fragmented and that the inner packet fits the computed
115	  budget. (Runs on hardware with `/dev/net/tun`; compiled under `-tags e2e`.)
116

# P1 MTU accounting and MSS-clamping guidance (T12)

The multipath Bind (`internal/bind`, T12) wraps every opaque WireGuard datagram
in an outer DATA frame before it leaves a per-path socket. That outer frame is
pure overhead the P0 pass-through Bind did not add, so the tunnel MTU must shrink
to keep a full-size inner packet from fragmenting on the wire. This document
records the arithmetic (the source of truth is `internal/bind/mtu.go`, pinned by
`TestInnerMTUFixture`) and the MSS-clamping the operator must apply so TCP stays
inside the budget.

## Why fragmentation must be avoided

- **Loss amplification.** An IP-fragmented outer datagram is only delivered if
  *every* fragment arrives. On a lossy WAN (Starlink/5G — the target links) the
  probability that all fragments survive is strictly worse than for a single
  datagram, so fragmentation inflates the effective loss rate exactly where we
  can least afford it.
- **PMTUD black holes.** Path-MTU discovery relies on ICMP "fragmentation
  needed" (IPv4 DF) / "packet too big" (IPv6). Consumer CPE and carrier NAT
  routinely filter that ICMP, so an oversized packet is silently dropped and the
  sender never learns why — the tunnel stalls for large flows while small packets
  pass. Sizing the inner MTU conservatively side-steps PMTUD entirely.

## The overhead stack

A tunnelled inner packet is wrapped in four nested layers before the wire:

```
[ outer IP | outer UDP | outer DATA frame | WG transport | inner IP payload ]
  \_______ 28 (IPv4) _______/ \___ 39 ___/ \____ 32 ____/ \___ inner MTU ___/
```

| Layer | Bytes | Constant |
| ----- | ----- | -------- |
| Outer IPv4 header | 20 | `IPv4UDPOverhead` (with UDP) |
| Outer UDP header | 8 | " |
| Outer DATA frame | **39** | `frame.DataOverhead` |
| WireGuard transport | **32** | `WGTransportOverhead` |

The DATA-frame overhead of **39 bytes** decomposes as: XChaCha20 nonce (24) +
kind discriminant (1) + outer-seq (8) + path-id (1) + fec-group (4) + flags (1).
DATA frames are unauthenticated (the inner WireGuard layer authenticates the real
payload), so they carry **no** MAC tag — the 39-byte figure is exact.
`TestDataOverheadMatchesEncoding` pins it to the real encoded length so the MTU
budget can never silently drift from the codec.

The WireGuard transport overhead of **32 bytes** is the 16-byte data-message
header (message type + reserved + receiver index + counter) plus the 16-byte
Poly1305 tag.

> Amnezia junk **prefixes** add further *variable* bytes on top of a real
> transport packet when obfuscation is configured. They are not subtracted here
> (they are per-packet variable and configurable); an amnezia deployment must
> lower the path MTU it feeds `InnerMTU` by its worst-case junk-prefix size, or
> accept that the largest junked packets may fragment. Wiring amnezia end-to-end
> is T19; this note is revisited there.

## Computed inner MTU

For the default 1500-byte IPv4 path MTU:

```
inner MTU = 1500 − 28 (IP+UDP) − 39 (DATA frame) − 32 (WG) = 1401 bytes
```

`internal/bind.InnerMTU(1500) == 1401`, asserted by `TestInnerMTUFixture`, and
`internal/device` sizes the TUN to exactly this value. An IPv6 underlay costs 20
more header bytes → `InnerMTU6(1500) == 1381`. Deployments whose real path MTU is
below 1500 (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks — PPPoE is 1492, and CGNAT/telco tunnels are
often lower) must lower the path MTU passed to `InnerMTU`; when paths differ, size
the tunnel to the **smallest** path's inner MTU, since the single virtual endpoint
carries one MTU for the whole bond.

## MSS clamping (the operator action)

Setting the TUN MTU bounds what the *local* stack originates, but TCP endpoints
*behind* the tunnel (a LAN host routed through the edge, or the concentrator's
downstream) negotiate their MSS from their own link MTU and will happily send
1460-byte segments that no longer fit once wrapped. The fix is to clamp the TCP
MSS of forwarded SYNs to the tunnel's inner MTU minus the inner IP+TCP headers:

```
MSS = inner MTU − 40 (IPv4: 20 IP + 20 TCP) = 1401 − 40 = 1361 bytes
      inner MTU − 60 (IPv6: 40 IP + 20 TCP) = 1381 − 60 = 1321 bytes
```

Clamp on the forwarding node (edge and concentrator) with the standard
`TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu` target on the tunnel interface, or an explicit value:

```sh
# Clamp to the discovered PMTU (preferred — tracks the tunnel MTU automatically):
iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
          -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu
ip6tables -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
          -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu

# Or pin an explicit MSS if a fixed lower path MTU is known:
iptables  -t mangle -A FORWARD -o wanbond0 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN \
          -j TCPMSS --set-mss 1361
```

`--clamp-mss-to-pmtu` is preferred: it derives the MSS from the tunnel interface
MTU, so it stays correct if the inner MTU is retuned (e.g. amnezia enabled, or a
lower real path MTU). UDP and other non-TCP traffic cannot be MSS-clamped; those
flows must rely on the inner MTU and, for locally originated traffic, on the
sender honouring the TUN MTU.

## What T12 verifies

- `TestInnerMTUFixture` / `TestDataOverheadMatchesEncoding` — the arithmetic and
  its coupling to the codec.
- The e2e `TestMultipathNoFragmentation` fixture sends a max-inner-MTU payload
  with DF set and asserts, from a packet capture on the edge egress, that no
  outer datagram is IP-fragmented and that the inner packet fits the computed
  budget. (Runs on hardware with `/dev/net/tun`; compiled under `-tags e2e`.)

3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"

8	// MTU accounting for the bonded datapath.
9	//
10	// A tunnelled application packet is wrapped in four nested layers before it hits
11	// the wire, each adding fixed overhead:
12	//
13	//		[ IP | UDP | outer DATA frame | WG transport | inner IP payload ]
14	//		  \___ IPv4UDPOverhead ___/   \_DataOverhead_/\_WGTransportOverhead_/
15	//
16	//	  - IP + UDP: the underlay carrying our outer datagram.
17	//	  - outer DATA frame: the bonding codec's nonce + DATA header (frame.DataOverhead).
18	//	  - WG transport: WireGuard's own data-message header + Poly1305 tag.
19	//
20	// InnerMTU sizes the TUN so a full-MTU inner packet, once wrapped in all four
21	// layers, still fits the path MTU without IP fragmentation. Fragmentation must be
22	// avoided: a fragment lost on a lossy WAN drops the whole datagram, and PMTUD
23	// black holes (ICMP "fragmentation needed" filtered by a middlebox) silently stall
24	// the tunnel. See docs/p1-mtu.md for the derivation and the MSS-clamping guidance
25	// that keeps TCP inside this budget.
26	const (
27		// DefaultPathMTU is the assumed underlay path MTU when none is measured. 1500
28		// is the near-universal Ethernet/most-broadband figure; deployments on links
29		// with a smaller MTU (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks) must lower it.
30		DefaultPathMTU = 1500
31	
32		// IPv4UDPOverhead is the outer IPv4 (20) + UDP (8) header cost. IPv4 is the
33		// conservative default here; an IPv6 underlay costs 20 bytes more (see
34		// IPv6UDPOverhead) so sizing for IPv4 and running over IPv6 only wastes a few
35		// bytes, never fragments.
36		IPv4UDPOverhead = 20 + 8
37	
38		// IPv6UDPOverhead is the outer IPv6 (40) + UDP (8) header cost.
39		IPv6UDPOverhead = 40 + 8
40	
41		// WGTransportOverhead is WireGuard's per-datagram data-message overhead: the
42		// 16-byte transport header (msg type + reserved + receiver index + counter)
43		// plus the 16-byte Poly1305 authentication tag. Amnezia junk PREFIXES add
44		// further variable bytes on top; they are handled as best-effort headroom, not
45		// subtracted here, because they are configurable and per-packet variable.
46		WGTransportOverhead = 16 + 16
47	)
48	
49	// FECParityMTUPenalty is the extra bytes a full-size FEC PARITY frame occupies on the
50	// wire over a DATA frame carrying the same-size inner payload (T24). A parity frame is
51	// nonce + PARITY header + shard payload, where the shard payload is the codec's length
52	// prefix + the FEC outer-seq prefix + the largest coded inner payload; a data frame is
53	// nonce + DATA header + that same inner payload. So the delta is:
54	//
55	//	(ParityOverhead + ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen) - DataOverhead  ==  5 bytes.
56	//
57	// When FEC is enabled the inner MTU is reduced by this penalty so a full-MTU DATA frame
58	// AND its group's PARITY frame both fit the path MTU — otherwise parity exceeds the
59	// path MTU by exactly this much and IP-fragments or is EMSGSIZE/PMTUD-blackholed, which
60	// silently kills the very redundancy FEC exists to provide on bulk full-size traffic.
61	const FECParityMTUPenalty = frame.ParityOverhead + fec.ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen - frame.DataOverhead
62	
63	// InnerMTU returns the largest inner (TUN) MTU that avoids IP fragmentation over
64	// an IPv4 underlay of the given path MTU: pathMTU minus the IP+UDP, outer DATA
65	// frame, and WireGuard transport overheads. fecEnabled additionally reserves the
66	// parity-over-data delta so a full-size parity frame also fits (see
67	// FECParityMTUPenalty); pass false when FEC is off to keep the pre-T24 budget.
68	func InnerMTU(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
69		return pathMTU - IPv4UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
70	}
71	
72	// InnerMTU6 is InnerMTU for an IPv6 underlay.
73	func InnerMTU6(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
74		return pathMTU - IPv6UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
75	}
76	
77	// fecPenalty is the inner-MTU reduction to reserve for FEC parity, or 0 when FEC is
78	// off.
79	func fecPenalty(fecEnabled bool) int {
80		if fecEnabled {
81			return FECParityMTUPenalty
82		}
83		return 0
84	}

```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"syscall"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13	)
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

```go
1	package log
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"io"
6		"log/slog"
7		"strings"
8	)
9	
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)
16	
17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.
26		Component(name string) Logger
27		// Path returns a child logger tagged with the given path name.
28		Path(name string) Logger
29	}
30	
31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}
35	
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
72		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldPath, name)}
73	}

21	// layers, still fits the path MTU without IP fragmentation. Fragmentation must be
22	// avoided: a fragment lost on a lossy WAN drops the whole datagram, and PMTUD
23	// black holes (ICMP "fragmentation needed" filtered by a middlebox) silently stall
24	// the tunnel. See docs/p1-mtu.md for the derivation and the MSS-clamping guidance
25	// that keeps TCP inside this budget.
26	const (
27		// DefaultPathMTU is the assumed underlay path MTU when none is measured. 1500
28		// is the near-universal Ethernet/most-broadband figure; deployments on links
29		// with a smaller MTU (some LTE/PPPoE uplinks) must lower it.
30		DefaultPathMTU = 1500
31	
32		// IPv4UDPOverhead is the outer IPv4 (20) + UDP (8) header cost. IPv4 is the
33		// conservative default here; an IPv6 underlay costs 20 bytes more (see
34		// IPv6UDPOverhead) so sizing for IPv4 and running over IPv6 only wastes a few
35		// bytes, never fragments.
36		IPv4UDPOverhead = 20 + 8
37	
38		// IPv6UDPOverhead is the outer IPv6 (40) + UDP (8) header cost.
39		IPv6UDPOverhead = 40 + 8
40	
41		// WGTransportOverhead is WireGuard's per-datagram data-message overhead: the
42		// 16-byte transport header (msg type + reserved + receiver index + counter)
43		// plus the 16-byte Poly1305 authentication tag. Amnezia junk PREFIXES add
44		// further variable bytes on top; they are handled as best-effort headroom, not
45		// subtracted here, because they are configurable and per-packet variable.
46		WGTransportOverhead = 16 + 16
47	)
48	
49	// FECParityMTUPenalty is the extra bytes a full-size FEC PARITY frame occupies on the
50	// wire over a DATA frame carrying the same-size inner payload (T24). A parity frame is
51	// nonce + PARITY header + shard payload, where the shard payload is the codec's length
52	// prefix + the FEC outer-seq prefix + the largest coded inner payload; a data frame is
53	// nonce + DATA header + that same inner payload. So the delta is:
54	//
55	//	(ParityOverhead + ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen) - DataOverhead  ==  5 bytes.
56	//
57	// When FEC is enabled the inner MTU is reduced by this penalty so a full-MTU DATA frame
58	// AND its group's PARITY frame both fit the path MTU — otherwise parity exceeds the
59	// path MTU by exactly this much and IP-fragments or is EMSGSIZE/PMTUD-blackholed, which
60	// silently kills the very redundancy FEC exists to provide on bulk full-size traffic.
61	const FECParityMTUPenalty = frame.ParityOverhead + fec.ShardFramingOverhead + fecSeqPrefixLen - frame.DataOverhead
62	
63	// InnerMTU returns the largest inner (TUN) MTU that avoids IP fragmentation over
64	// an IPv4 underlay of the given path MTU: pathMTU minus the IP+UDP, outer DATA
65	// frame, and WireGuard transport overheads. fecEnabled additionally reserves the
66	// parity-over-data delta so a full-size parity frame also fits (see
67	// FECParityMTUPenalty); pass false when FEC is off to keep the pre-T24 budget.
68	func InnerMTU(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
69		return pathMTU - IPv4UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
70	}
71	
72	// InnerMTU6 is InnerMTU for an IPv6 underlay.
73	func InnerMTU6(pathMTU int, fecEnabled bool) int {
74		return pathMTU - IPv6UDPOverhead - frame.DataOverhead - WGTransportOverhead - fecPenalty(fecEnabled)
75	}
76	
77	// fecPenalty is the inner-MTU reduction to reserve for FEC parity, or 0 when FEC is
78	// off.
79	func fecPenalty(fecEnabled bool) int {
80		if fecEnabled {
81			return FECParityMTUPenalty
82		}
83		return 0
84	}
```

57	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the

63		// routePrefixes is the default-route wiring installed into wanbond0 for a
64		// mode=default-route peer (I6, Q41): the wg-quick-style split of that peer's
65		// allowed_ips (the two /1s for a /0), installed after dev.Up and withdrawn on
66		// Close. Empty (nil) for a config with no default-route peer, so a plain tunnel
67		// programs — and tears down — no routes at all.
68		routePrefixes []netip.Prefix
69		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
70		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
71		bind *bind.Multipath
72		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
73		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
74		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
75		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
76		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
77		cfg *config.Config
78		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
79		reloadMu sync.Mutex
80		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
81		log log.Logger
82		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
83		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
84		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
85		stopProbes func()
86		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
87		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
88		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
89		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
90		stopReconcile func()
91		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
92		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
93		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
94		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
95		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
96		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
97		stopHubFailover func()
98		// stopResolution halts the edge-side DNS re-resolution loop (T74): it re-resolves each
99		// opted-in hostname peer-endpoint spec on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-band on hub loss,
100		// feeding fresh records to the hub-failover controller. Close calls it between stopHubFailover
101		// and dev.Close. It is idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, an all-literal
102		// edge, or when no hostname spec is configured — Q29 inertness).
103		stopResolution func()
104		// stopSession halts the WG-session monitor loop (I2): it polls the engine's last-handshake
105		// state at probe cadence and emits ONE INFO 'session established' record on each 0->1 edge.
106		// Close calls it before dev.Close so no IpcGet races the engine teardown. It is idempotent
107		// and never nil.
108		stopSession func()
109		// amnezia is the obfuscation profile this tunnel holds against the
110		// process-global amnezia guard (see amneziaGuard); Close releases it.
111		amnezia     config.Amnezia
112		releaseOnce sync.Once
113	
114		// metricsSrc is the live metrics.Source over the Bind; it is stable for the tunnel's
115		// life (the Bind pointer never changes), so a reload that rebinds the endpoint reuses
116		// the SAME Source — its derived-throughput last-sample state survives the rebind.
117		metricsSrc metrics.Source
118		// metricsSrv is the running /metrics endpoint, nil when [metrics].listen is empty.
119		// It is (re)assigned by applyMetricsLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so
120		// a SIGHUP-driven rebind never races shutdown. metricsListen mirrors the address it
121		// is bound to so a reload can detect a listen change without inspecting the server.
122		metricsSrv    *metrics.Server
123		metricsListen string

**`internal/device/tundiag.go`** — tunDiagnosticInterval(constant), diagnosingTUN(struct), diagnoseEIO(method), Write(method), newDiagnosingTUN(function)

```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"syscall"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13	)
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}

**`internal/log/log.go`** — Info(method), Component(method), Logger(interface), Warn(method)

```go
1	package log
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"io"
6		"log/slog"
7		"strings"
8	)
9	
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)
16	
17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.
26		Component(name string) Logger
27		// Path returns a child logger tagged with the given path name.
28		Path(name string) Logger
29	}
30	
31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}
35	
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
72		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldPath, name)}
73	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
8	)
9	
10	// Default WireGuard message-type discriminants and the keepalive size, mirrored from
11	// amneziawg-go/device (noise-protocol.go) rather than imported: the bind depends on the
12	// conn package only, and pulling the whole device package in for these constants would
13	// couple the transport to the engine's platform/init machinery. The type is the
14	// little-endian uint32 at the head of the message; the keepalive is a transport message
15	// with empty content — MessageTransportHeaderSize (16) + poly1305 tag (16). Kept in
16	// lockstep with device.Default*Type / device.MessageKeepaliveSize.
17	const (
18		wgMessageInitiationType  uint32 = 1 // handshake initiation (device.DefaultMessageInitiationType)
19		wgMessageResponseType    uint32 = 2 // handshake response (device.DefaultMessageResponseType)
20		wgMessageCookieReplyType uint32 = 3 // cookie reply (device.DefaultMessageCookieReplyType)
21		wgMessageTransportType   uint32 = 4 // transport data / keepalive (device.DefaultMessageTransportType)
22	
23		// wgTypeLen is the length of the leading little-endian uint32 message type.
24		wgTypeLen = 4
25		// wgKeepaliveSize is the on-wire size of a WireGuard keepalive (device.MessageKeepaliveSize
26		// == MessageTransportSize). AmneziaWG does NOT junk-prefix transport frames, so a
27		// keepalive stays this size under obfuscation; a transport frame longer than this
28		// carries tunnelled payload and is bulk data.
29		wgKeepaliveSize = 32
30		// wgMagicHeaderMin is amneziawg-go's threshold for a custom magic header taking effect:
31		// device.go applies a configured header only when it is > 4, otherwise the default type
32		// word stands (so a header of 1..4 is a no-op and the wire word is the default). The
33		// classifier mirrors that rule exactly so it matches what is actually on the wire.
34		wgMagicHeaderMin uint32 = 4
35	)
36	
37	// wgClassifier maps an outbound WireGuard datagram to its pacer traffic class (defect
38	// D22), parameterized by the tunnel's AmneziaWG obfuscation profile so classification is
39	// correct in advanced-security mode — wanbond's PRIMARY deployment — and not only in
40	// vanilla WireGuard. AmneziaWG breaks a hardcoded classifier two ways (amneziawg-go
41	// send.go): (a) custom magic headers h1..h4 replace the literal 1/2/3/4 type words, and
42	// (b) s1/s2 junk bytes are PREPENDED to handshake initiation/response datagrams, shifting
43	// the type word off offset 0. This classifier holds the CONFIGURED type words and junk
44	// prefix lengths and reads the type word at the right offset for each candidate type.
45	//
46	// A zero (unconfigured) profile yields the vanilla classifier: default type words, no
47	// junk prefix.
48	type wgClassifier struct {
49		initType      uint32 // effective handshake-initiation type word (h1, or default)
50		responseType  uint32 // effective handshake-response type word (h2, or default)
51		cookieType    uint32 // effective cookie-reply type word (h3, or default)
52		transportType uint32 // effective transport (data/keepalive) type word (h4, or default)
53		initJunk      int    // s1: junk bytes prepended before an initiation packet's type word
54		responseJunk  int    // s2: junk bytes prepended before a response packet's type word
55	}
56	
57	// newWGClassifier builds the classifier from the tunnel's Amnezia profile. It mirrors
58	// amneziawg-go's ">4 or default" magic-header rule (device.go) so the type words match
59	// the wire exactly, and takes s1/s2 as the initiation/response junk-prefix lengths. An
60	// unconfigured (zero-value) profile produces the vanilla classifier.
61	func newWGClassifier(a config.Amnezia) wgClassifier {
62		return wgClassifier{
63			initType:      effectiveMagic(a.H1, wgMessageInitiationType),
64			responseType:  effectiveMagic(a.H2, wgMessageResponseType),
65			cookieType:    effectiveMagic(a.H3, wgMessageCookieReplyType),
66			transportType: effectiveMagic(a.H4, wgMessageTransportType),
67			initJunk:      junkLen(a.S1),
68			responseJunk:  junkLen(a.S2),
69		}
70	}
71	
72	// effectiveMagic returns the type word actually placed on the wire for a header: the
73	// configured value when it exceeds wgMagicHeaderMin (amneziawg-go's activation rule),
74	// otherwise the default. This matches device.go, where a header of 1..4 is ignored.
75	func effectiveMagic(configured, def uint32) uint32 {
76		if configured > wgMagicHeaderMin {
77			return configured
78		}
79		return def
80	}
81	
82	// junkLen clamps a junk-prefix length to a non-negative offset (a zero/omitted profile
83	// has no prefix; config validation forbids a negative value).
84	func junkLen(s int) int {
85		if s < 0 {
86			return 0
87		}
88		return s
89	}
90	
91	// classify maps one outbound WireGuard message to its pacer traffic class. Order matters
92	// for the safety property "DATA never classifies as control": a transport frame leads
93	// with the transport type word at offset 0 and is checked FIRST, so a bulk data frame
94	// (transport, size > keepalive) always resolves to ClassData before any control branch is
95	// considered. Handshake initiation/response type words are read at their junk-shifted
96	// offsets (s1/s2). A frame that matches nothing — including AmneziaWG junk packets — is
97	// data (fail toward pacing; an unrecognised frame must not gain the control-class bypass).
98	func (c wgClassifier) classify(pkt []byte) sched.FrameClass {
99		// Transport (data or keepalive): type word at offset 0, never junk-prefixed. Only a
100		// keepalive-sized transport is a control frame; anything larger is tunnelled bulk data.
101		if t, ok := readType(pkt, 0); ok && t == c.transportType {
102			if len(pkt) == wgKeepaliveSize {
103				return sched.ClassControl
104			}
105			return sched.ClassData
106		}
107		// Cookie reply: type word at offset 0, not junk-prefixed.
108		if t, ok := readType(pkt, 0); ok && t == c.cookieType {
109			return sched.ClassControl
110		}
111		// Handshake initiation: type word after s1 junk bytes.
112		if t, ok := readType(pkt, c.initJunk); ok && t == c.initType {
113			return sched.ClassControl
114		}
115		// Handshake response: type word after s2 junk bytes.
116		if t, ok := readType(pkt, c.responseJunk); ok && t == c.responseType {
117			return sched.ClassControl
118		}
119		return sched.ClassData
120	}
121	
122	// classifyBatch returns the pacer traffic class for a batch of WireGuard datagrams handed
123	// to Send in one call. The batch is classified ClassControl when ANY buffer is a control
124	// frame: the batch shares one Pick (one path selection / one pacing decision), so biasing
125	// a mixed batch toward the control class keeps a co-batched handshake/keepalive out of the
126	// shed set — the conservative, rekey-favouring choice. In practice WireGuard emits control
127	// frames in their own Send calls, so a mixed batch is rare. An empty batch is data.
128	func (c wgClassifier) classifyBatch(bufs [][]byte) sched.FrameClass {
129		for _, b := range bufs {
130			if c.classify(b) == sched.ClassControl {
131				return sched.ClassControl
132			}
133		}
134		return sched.ClassData
135	}
136	
137	// readType reads the little-endian uint32 message-type word at byte offset off, reporting
138	// ok=false when the buffer is too short to hold a type word at that offset.
139	func readType(pkt []byte, off int) (uint32, bool) {
140		if off < 0 || off+wgTypeLen > len(pkt) {
141			return 0, false
142		}
143		return binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(pkt[off : off+wgTypeLen]), true
144	}

1	# wanbond — install and operations
2	
3	wanbond ships as a single static binary. One binary serves both roles — the
4	**edge** (the mobile Linux box bonding the WAN uplinks) and the **concentrator**
5	(the public-IP VPS terminating the tunnel); the role is selected by the `role`
6	key in the config file, never by which binary is invoked.
7	
8	> **Provisioning a fresh deployment end-to-end?** Follow the
9	> **[pre-pilot rollout runbook](runbook.md)** — a top-to-bottom operator
10	> procedure (key/PSK generation, both-ends config, standby-concentrator hub
11	> failover, firewall persistence, pacing, and health checks) that ties the
12	> sections below together. This document is the per-topic reference the runbook
13	> points back into.
14	
15	## 1. Build the release binaries
16	
17	From the repo root, inside the dev shell:
18	
19	```sh
20	nix develop -c just release
21	```
22	
23	This cross-compiles `cmd/wanbond` with `CGO_ENABLED=0` (fully static, no libc
24	dependency) for both deployment architectures into `dist/`:
25	
26	- `dist/wanbond-linux-amd64` — x86_64 (typical OCI/AWS VPS concentrator)
27	- `dist/wanbond-linux-arm64` — aarch64 (ARM edge boxes, Ampere VPSes)
28	
29	Verify with `file dist/*`: both must report `statically linked`. Alternatively
30	`nix build` produces the host-architecture binary via the flake package.
31	
32	## 2. Install the binary
33	
34	On each host (pick the artifact matching `uname -m`):
35	
36	```sh
37	install -m 0755 wanbond-linux-<arch> /usr/local/bin/wanbond
38	wanbond version   # prints the stamped build version
39	```
40	
41	## 3. Write the config file — 0600 REQUIRED
42	
43	The daemon takes exactly one flag: `--config <path>`. The systemd units use
44	`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml` and `/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`.
45	
46	The file holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so
47	`config.Load` **refuses any file whose permission bits are not exactly `0600`**
48	(`insecure permissions` error at startup). Create it as:
49	
50	```sh
51	mkdir -p /etc/wanbond
52	touch /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
53	chown root:root /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
54	chmod 0600 /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
55	```
56	
57	### Edge config (`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml`)
58	
59	```toml
60	role = "edge"
61	psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"
62	
63	# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
64	# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
65	[[paths]]
66	name = "starlink"
67	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
68	
69	[[paths]]
70	name = "5g"
71	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
72	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
73	                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks
74	
75	[wireguard]
76	private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"
77	
78	[[wireguard.peers]]
79	public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
80	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge; an ip:port literal
81	                                   # (default) OR a hostname:port behind the
82	                                   # opt-in `dns = true` below — see §Optional
83	                                   # [dns] resolver block.
84	# dns = true                        # OPTIONAL, default false. Enables hostname
85	# resolution for the endpoint above (e.g. endpoint = "hub.example.com:51820").
86	# The [dns] block is itself OPTIONAL: absent, hostnames resolve through the
87	# OS system resolver; see §Optional [dns] resolver block below.
88	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
89	# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
90	# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
91	# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
92	# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.
93	# Each entry may likewise be an ip:port literal or, with `dns = true`, a
94	# hostname:port. Hub failover (all-paths-down detection + switch/re-handshake
95	# to the next endpoint) is implemented: T54 config surface + T57 switch.
96	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # concentrator's inner tunnel address
97	
98	[metrics]
99	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # loopback only; the daemon rejects anything else
100	
101	[log]
102	level = "info"
103	```
104	
105	### Concentrator config (`/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`)
106	
107	```toml
108	role = "concentrator"
109	psk = "<same PSK as the edge>"
110	
111	# The concentrator learns the edge's real per-path endpoints from
112	# authenticated traffic; it only needs its own bind address.
113	[[paths]]
114	name = "wan0"
115	source_addr = "10.0.0.5"           # the VPS's primary (private) interface IP
116	
117	[wireguard]
118	private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
119	listen_port = 51820                # required on the concentrator
120	
121	[[wireguard.peers]]
122	public_key = "<base64 edge public key>"
123	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32"]     # edge's inner tunnel address; no endpoint —
124	                                   # the concentrator roams the edge dynamically
125	
126	[metrics]
127	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
128	
129	[log]
130	level = "info"
131	```
132	
133	Optional on both ends: an `[amnezia]` obfuscation block (all-or-nothing — when
134	used, `jc`, `jmin`, `jmax`, `s1`, `s2` must all be set, identically on both
135	ends). Omit it entirely for plain WireGuard framing.
136	
137	Generate keys with standard WireGuard tooling (`wg genkey | tee k | wg pubkey`)
138	and the PSK with `head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64`.
139	
140	### Multi-peer concentrator (G4) — supporting multiple edges
141	
142	A concentrator can bond traffic from **multiple edges** (e.g., a branch-office
143	edge, a mobile edge, a cloud gateway). Plural `[[wireguard.peers]]` blocks are
144	supported; with more than one peer, each edge authenticates with its own psk
145	for authenticated source-based demux:
146	
147	```toml
148	role = "concentrator"
149	psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"       # REQUIRED by validation; authenticates no
150	                                   # peer once a second peer is configured
151	                                   # (see note below)
152	
153	[[paths]]
154	name = "wan0"
155	source_addr = "10.0.0.5"
156	
157	[wireguard]
158	private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
159	listen_port = 51820
160	
161	# Edge 1: office, its own PSK
162	[[wireguard.peers]]
163	public_key = "<base64 office-edge public key>"
164	allowed_ips = ["10.77.1.0/24"]
165	psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for office-edge>"   # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
166	name = "office"                                 # REQUIRED in multi-peer mode
167	
168	# Edge 2: mobile, its own PSK
169	[[wireguard.peers]]
170	public_key = "<base64 mobile-edge public key>"
171	allowed_ips = ["10.77.2.0/24"]
172	psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK for mobile-edge>"   # Must differ from office's psk
173	name = "mobile"                                 # Must differ from office's name
174	
175	[metrics]
176	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
177	```
178	
179	**Single-peer back-compatibility:** when a concentrator has only one peer
180	(`[[wireguard.peers]]`), a per-peer `psk` is **rejected** at config load — leave
181	it unset; the top-level `psk` remains the sole authenticator, so existing
182	single-peer configs parse and run unchanged. A per-peer `name` is optional and
183	has no effect on metrics for a single bound peer (the `peer` label is omitted
184	entirely, not emitted empty).
185	
186	**Multi-peer requirements and authentication:** with more than one peer, each
187	peer **must** have both a unique `psk` (config load rejects a duplicate) and a
188	unique `name`. Every peer authenticates its OWN PROBE frames with its own psk;
189	the concentrator learns each source address's owning peer only from a PROBE
190	that MAC-verifies under that peer's psk, and subsequent DATA/PARITY frames from
191	that source route accordingly without re-authentication. **The top-level `psk`
192	stays required by config validation in every configuration, but on a
193	multi-peer concentrator it authenticates no peer** — an existing single-peer
194	edge does NOT keep authenticating via the top-level psk once a second peer is
195	added; it must be given its own per-peer psk at that point.
196	
197	**Metrics `peer` label:** additional peers (all but the first-configured) are
198	exposed under their configured `name` as the metrics `peer` label. The
199	first/primary peer's series always carry the empty label `peer=""`, even
200	though its `name` is set and required in multi-peer mode — this is the current
201	shipped behavior (defect D58), not something this doc changes.
202	
203	**Edges see no change:** each edge always points to the concentrator's public
204	address (its public IP + port); it does not know or care that multiple edges
205	are connected to the same concentrator. The concentrator's internal demux is
206	entirely transparent to the edge.
207	
208	### Optional `[fec]` forward-error-correction plane
209	
210	FEC is **off** unless an `[fec]` block is present. A fixed-ratio block protects
211	each group of `data_shards` (K) inner datagrams with `parity_shards` (M) parity
212	frames at a constant M/K overhead:
213	
214	```toml
215	[fec]
216	enabled = true
217	data_shards = 10
218	parity_shards = 6        # in adaptive mode this is the parity CEILING
219	# adaptive = true        # opt into the closed-loop controller (below)
220	# target_residual = 0.005  # residual-loss SLA — the recommended adaptive surface
221	# safety_factor = 4.0    # legacy headroom multiplier (mutually exclusive with target_residual)
222	```
223	
224	With `adaptive = true` the send side runs the loss-tracking controller: the
225	per-group parity floats in `[0, parity_shards]` to match measured path loss, so
226	a clean path spends near-zero overhead. Two mutually-exclusive ways size that
227	parity — **set exactly one**:
228	
229	- **`target_residual`** (recommended, the primary surface): the target
230	  **post-recovery residual-loss** fraction in `(0,1)`. The controller derives the
231	  minimum parity M whose modeled binomial residual `E[max(0,D-M)]/K`
232	  (`D ~ Bin(K, smoothed loss)`) is at/below this target for the current loss and
233	  K, capped at the `parity_shards` ceiling. It maps an operator's loss budget
234	  directly to redundancy: e.g. `target_residual = 0.005` holds the post-recovery
235	  loss at/below 0.5% (the P4 bound) as long as the ceiling allows. Raising the
236	  ceiling (`parity_shards`) lets a tighter target be met under heavier loss.
237	- **`safety_factor`** (legacy): a bare headroom multiplier ≥ 1 sizing M so
238	  `M/(K+M) ≥ safety_factor × loss`. It does **not** map to a residual bound — an
239	  operator must hand-tune it per (loss, K) to clear a given SLA (the reason
240	  `target_residual` supersedes it). Defaults to 1.5 when adaptive and neither
241	  field is set; note 1.5 sizes M=1 at 5% loss with K=10 (~1% residual), so a
242	  sub-1% SLA needs a higher factor **or**, preferably, `target_residual`.
243	
244	Setting both `target_residual` and `safety_factor` is rejected at config load.
245	
246	### Optional `[scheduler]` weighted aggregation + pacing
247	
248	The send scheduler defaults to **active-backup** (one active path, instant
249	failover). An optional `[scheduler]` block selects the **weighted-aggregation**
250	policy and, off by default, per-path send-**pacing** (token buckets that bound
251	bufferbloat under sustained load):
252	
253	```toml
254	[scheduler]
255	policy = "weighted"
256	pacing_enabled = true     # OFF by default; when on, size the pace from the links below
257	```
258	
259	When pacing is enabled you may declare each uplink's bandwidth and baseline RTT
260	directly on its `[[paths]]` block; the daemon then sizes the per-path pace from the
261	bandwidth-delay product at config load, instead of the synthetic default:
262	
263	```toml
264	[[paths]]
265	name = "starlink"
266	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
267	link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"  # SI bit/s: k/M/G = 1e3/1e6/1e9 (e.g. "10Mbit", "1Gbit")
268	link_rtt = "45ms"          # baseline RTT — the delay term of the pacing burst
269	```
270	
271	- The declaration is **operator-declared, not auto-tuned**: the value is fixed at
272	  load; wanbond does not adjust it live. Measure it once per link.
273	- It is **all-or-nothing**: declare `link_bandwidth` (and `link_rtt`) on *every*
274	  path or none. The shared per-path pace is sized to the **slowest declared link**
275	  (the bottleneck), so a partial declaration is rejected.
276	- A declared bandwidth with `pacing_enabled = false` (the default) is **inert** —
277	  the synthetic default pace is kept and the tunnel behaves as before.
278	- `link_bandwidth` is **mutually exclusive** with the raw `per_path_capacity_fps` /
279	  `pacing_burst_frames` knobs: declare the link bandwidth *or* set the frame-slot
280	  knobs, not both. A non-positive or unparseable bandwidth/RTT is rejected at load.
281	
282	### Optional `[dns]` resolver block
283	
284	Hostname peer endpoints (opt-in per peer with `dns = true`, see the endpoint
285	list note above) are resolved through the OS **system resolver by default** —
286	an absent `[dns]` block is inert. To route that resolution through a private
287	DNS-over-HTTPS or DNS-over-TLS resolver instead, add an explicit `[dns]`
288	block:
289	
290	```toml
291	[dns]
292	resolver = "doh"                                # "system" (default) | "doh" | "dot"
293	doh_url = "https://198.51.100.1/dns-query"       # required iff resolver = "doh"
294	# dot_server = "198.51.100.1"                    # required iff resolver = "dot"
295	# poll_interval = "30s"                          # re-resolution cadence; must be > 0
296	# timeout = "5s"                                 # per-lookup bound; must be > 0
297	```
298	
299	- `[dns]` only **selects the transport** the per-peer `dns = true` opt-in uses;
300	  it never turns hostname resolution on by itself.
301	- **BOOTSTRAP-IP invariant**: `doh_url`/`dot_server`'s host must itself be
302	  reachable *without* a DNS lookup (otherwise resolving your private
303	  resolver's own name would need the very system resolver you configured it
304	  to avoid). Give it as an IP literal, or set `bootstrap_ip` explicitly when
305	  it is a hostname — config load fails fast otherwise. `bootstrap_ip` must
306	  stay absent when the host is already an IP literal; a non-empty value
307	  there is rejected at load as a mode mismatch.
308	- `dot_server` dials the fixed IANA-assigned DoT port (853); an explicit
309	  `host:port` form must use that exact port.
310	- `doh_url`/`dot_server` are mode-specific: setting the wrong one for the
311	  selected `resolver` (or either one under `resolver = "system"`) is
312	  rejected at load.
313	- **Tolerant boot**: a hostname endpoint that cannot be resolved at startup
314	  (resolver down, DNS outage) never fails bring-up — the tunnel comes up
315	  *without* that peer endpoint and the background re-resolution loop installs
316	  it and initiates the handshake on the first successful lookup. Steady-state
317	  re-resolution then repoints the bond whenever the record changes.
318	
319	### 3a. Tuning per-link bandwidth and pacing
320	
321	**Pacing ships DISABLED by default.** When enabled with `pacing_enabled = true`
322	under the `[scheduler]` block, wanbond sizes the per-path send-pace from the
323	bandwidth-delay product (BDP) — the product of each uplink's usable bandwidth and
324	round-trip latency — to bound bufferbloat (excessive queueing) under sustained load.
325	The declared bandwidth is **operator-measured, not auto-tuned**: you measure it once
326	per link and enter it in the config.
327	
328	This section describes how to measure the required values (`link_bandwidth` and
329	`link_rtt`), where to enter them, and how to verify pacing is effective.
330	
331	#### Step 1: Measure baseline (idle) round-trip time
332	
333	Measure the idle RTT on each path — the latency with light traffic:
334	
335	```sh
336	# From the edge, ping the concentrator's tunnel address (e.g., 10.77.0.1).
337	# Use -c 10 for a quick sample; ignore the first ping (ARP/cold cache).
338	ping -c 10 10.77.0.1
339	```
340	
341	Example output:
342	```
343	round-trip min/avg/max/stddev = 20.1/21.5/23.2/1.0 ms
344	```
345	
346	Record the **average** RTT (here: 21.5 ms). If RTTs are highly variable, the path
347	is jittery; take a longer sample (e.g. `-c 30`). Each path's idle RTT becomes the
348	`link_rtt` value in the config. If paths have different RTTs, declare each path's
349	own value separately.
350	
351	#### Step 2: Measure usable bandwidth per uplink
352	
353	You have two options:
354	
355	##### Option A: Measurement via the capped-fixture test (T52, netns)
356	
357	If you have access to a lab setup (or are developing/testing wanbond), the test
358	suite includes a deterministic bandwidth-measurement sub-test. From the repo root:
359	
360	```sh
361	# This runs the entire fixture-impairment suite, including the BDP sub-test:
362	go test -tags e2e -run TestFixtureImpairment -v ./test/e2e
363	```
364	
365	Toward the end of the output, you will see a `bdp` sub-test log (the numbers below
366	are **illustrative placeholders** — the sub-test is report-only and its measured
367	values vary run to run; read the actual figures from *your* run's log):
368	
369	```
370	path capped BDP: idle RTT=<e.g. ~5>ms loaded RTT=...ms (bufferbloat Δ=...ms) | 
371	  achieved throughput=<e.g. ~35-56> Mbit/s | BDP=... bytes (...frames @ ≈1540B/frame) | 
372	  SizePacingFromBDP -> capacityFPS=... burstFrames=...
373	```
374	
375	The **achieved throughput** (whatever your run reports) is a measured point;
376	the fixture builds a sustained queue by running iperf3 under a controlled bandwidth
377	cap, so it measures the true link-limited throughput (not CPU-bound). The per-frame
378	size (`≈1540B`) is the full path MTU (1500) plus the outer-frame DATA overhead
379	(40) that `SizePacingFromBDP` uses to convert bandwidth to a frame rate.
380	
381	##### Option B: Measurement on the real deployment (manual)
382	
383	Measure each uplink independently with iperf3, one at a time:
384	
385	```sh
386	# On the concentrator, start the iperf3 server:
387	iperf3 -s -B 10.77.0.1
388	
389	# On the edge, run a sustained transfer to build a standing queue (8–10 seconds):
390	# This measures the throughput the link will sustain under sustained load
391	# and allows RTT to stabilize under queue pressure.
392	iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 10
393	```
394	
395	Example output:
396	```
397	Bitrate         Jitter  Lost/Total Datagrams
398	...
399	  50.2 Mbit/s  ...     ...
400	```
401	
402	If using TCP (default), read the throughput from the final summary line.
403	
404	**Important:** Repeat this measurement for each uplink **separately** — bring up
405	only one path at a time, or isolate it at the router layer. The measurement must
406	reflect each link's independent capacity, not the bonded throughput.
407	
408	Also measure the RTT under load (as a sanity check on bufferbloat):
409	
410	```sh
411	# While iperf3 is running (in another terminal on the edge):
412	ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
413	```
414	
415	Record the average RTT under load. If it is much higher than the idle RTT
416	(e.g. idle 20 ms → loaded 200 ms), the path has severe bufferbloat; pacing will
417	help control this (that is the whole point).
418	
419	#### Step 3: Enter the measured values in the config
420	
421	For each path, add `link_bandwidth` and `link_rtt` to the `[[paths]]` block:
422	
423	```toml
424	[[paths]]
425	name = "starlink"
426	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
427	link_bandwidth = "50Mbit"    # from Step 2 measurement: 50.2 Mbit/s → round to 50Mbit
428	link_rtt = "21ms"            # from Step 1: 21.5 ms idle RTT → round to 21ms
429	
430	[[paths]]
431	name = "5g"
432	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
433	link_bandwidth = "10Mbit"    # measured as slower
434	link_rtt = "45ms"            # higher baseline latency
435	```
436	
437	**Rules:**
438	- **Declare on every path or none.** If you declare `link_bandwidth` on one path,
439	  you must declare it on all (wanbond sizes pacing to the slowest link, the
440	  bottleneck). Partial declarations are rejected at load.
441	- **Round conservatively.** Round down if unsure (e.g., measure 49.8 Mbit/s →
442	  declare `49Mbit` rather than `50Mbit`). Under-sized pacing is safe; over-sized
443	  pacing may not bind and bufferbloat may occur.
444	- **Use the `link_rtt` from Step 1**, not the loaded RTT from Step 2. The idle RTT
445	  is the baseline delay the BDP calculation assumes; the loaded RTT tells you how
446	  much bufferbloat exists.
447	
448	#### Step 4: Enable pacing and deploy
449	
450	Ensure the edge config has the weighted scheduler and pacing enabled:
451	
452	```toml
453	[scheduler]
454	policy = "weighted"
455	pacing_enabled = true
456	```
457	
458	Reload or restart the daemon:
459	
460	```sh
461	systemctl reload wanbond-edge   # SIGHUP: re-reads config, applies path changes
462	# or
463	systemctl restart wanbond-edge  # full restart if config is invalid
464	```
465	
466	Verify there are no errors in the journal:
467	
468	```sh
469	journalctl -u wanbond-edge -n 20
470	```
471	
472	A successful load will log `config loaded` (or, on reload, `config reloaded`);
473	if the daemon rejects the config (e.g. inconsistent bandwidth declarations,
474	unparseable values), the error message will say why.
475	
476	#### Step 5: Verify bufferbloat is controlled
477	
478	While running a sustained load (iperf3 from edge to concentrator), measure the
479	RTT under pacing:
480	
481	```sh
482	# Edge, run iperf3 traffic:
483	iperf3 -c 10.77.0.1 -t 30
484	
485	# Edge, another terminal, sample RTT under load:
486	ping -i 0.2 10.77.0.1
487	```
488	
489	If pacing is working, the RTT under sustained load should be **close to the idle
490	RTT** (ideally within 5–10 ms, depending on the link's buffering). Before pacing,
491	you may have seen loaded RTT inflate to 100+ ms on a bufferbloated link; pacing
492	bounds the queue so the inflation is minimal.
493	
494	**Note:** the test suite's netns fixture is CPU-bound and does not build the
495	standing queues needed to validate pacing against real links. Real-link
496	verification (this step) is essential: measure on your actual uplinks to confirm
497	pacing meets your bufferbloat target.
498	
499	### 3b. Policy-routing edge topologies: source-IP pinning with `bind = "source"`
500	
501	**Symptom:** On a VLAN-per-WAN edge with `ip rule from <source_addr>` policy routing,
502	the tunnel silently fails (ENETUNREACH on all packets), even though `ping -I <source_ip>`
503	proves the WAN interface works.
504	
505	**Root cause:** wanbond's default `bind` mode is `"auto"`, which selects `SO_BINDTODEVICE`
506	(device bind, wildcard source) on one-address interfaces — the exact case of a VLAN-per-WAN
507	edge. A wildcard-source socket never matches `ip rule from <source_addr>`, so the route
508	lookup falls through to the main table, finds no route to the concentrator via that VLAN,
509	and returns ENETUNREACH. The tunnel's UDP packets are silently dropped at the IP layer; no
510	error appears in the daemon's logs. This failure was observed in production (D38).
511	
512	**Workaround 1: Policy rule with output interface (production recipe):**
513	
514	Add an `oif <dev>` (output interface) clause to your policy rule **instead of** a
515	`from <source_ip>` clause. `oif` and `from` are ANDed together, not ORed — and a
516	wildcard-source device-bind socket, which is exactly the root cause above, never
517	matches `from <source_ip>`. The `from` clause would therefore make the rule just as
518	dead as the `ip rule from <source_addr>` rule that caused the symptom. Match on
519	output interface alone instead; that stays correct regardless of which source
520	address the socket ends up using:
521	
522	```sh
523	# Replace <dev> and <N> with your values.
524	# <dev>: the WAN interface name (e.g., eth0.231)
525	# <N>: the routing table number (e.g., 10)
526	
527	ip rule add oif <dev> table <N> prio 100
528	ip route add default dev <dev> via <gateway-ip> table <N>
529	```
530	
531	This is the recipe actually used to work around D38 in production. **It is not
532	reboot-persistent** — `ip rule`/`ip route` are runtime-only kernel state and do not
533	survive a reboot or an interface recreate, regardless of `ip rule` ordering. Persist
534	it the same way this document persists other runtime-only `ip`/`nft`/`iptables` state
535	elsewhere: fold the `ip rule add`/`ip route add` calls into the role's re-apply script
536	under the `wanbond-addressing@.service` oneshot (§4, "Persistence recipe for
537	non-networkd hosts"), or into whatever equivalent boot-time mechanism replays your
538	policy routing (cf. the `netfilter-persistent` pattern in §5 for the analogous
539	firewall-rule case).
540	
541	**Workaround 2: Per-path `bind = "source"` toggle (recommended):**
542	
543	Add `bind = "source"` to each `[[paths]]` block in the config. This forces the path socket
544	to bind to the source IP (pre-T16 behavior) instead of the device, ensuring the
545	wildcard-source collision never occurs. The socket's source IP will match your
546	`ip rule from` clauses:
547	
548	```toml
549	[[paths]]
550	name = "starlink"
551	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
552	bind = "source"
553	
554	[[paths]]
555	name = "5g"
556	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
557	bind = "source"
558	```
559	
560	If every path in a VLAN-per-WAN topology needs source binding, set the top-level
561	default instead of repeating `bind = "source"` on each `[[paths]]` block — a path
562	only needs its own `bind` when it deviates from the default:
563	
564	```toml
565	bind = "source"
566	
567	[[paths]]
568	name = "starlink"
569	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
570	
571	[[paths]]
572	name = "5g"
573	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
574	```
575	
576	Accepted `bind` values (top-level default, and per-path override) are:
577	
578	- `"source"` — force source-IP binding (defeats device-bind roam tolerance, safe for
579	  policy-routed topologies)
580	- `"device"` — force device binding (requires manual rule workaround for policy routing)
581	- `"auto"` (default) — automatic heuristic: device-bind only when the source resolves to
582	  a non-loopback interface, that interface carries exactly one address of the source's
583	  family, AND exactly one configured path resolves to that interface (two paths sharing
584	  one device fall back to source binding, since two wildcard+device sockets on the same
585	  port would collide `EADDRINUSE`); every other case source-binds.
586	
587	Restart the daemon after changing the config:
588	
589	```sh
590	systemctl restart wanbond-edge
591	```
592	
593	### 3z. Full configuration reference (all keys)
594	
595	This is the exhaustive key list — every configuration key wanbond reads, in one
596	place. The example below is **edge-oriented**; concentrator-only keys are shown
597	and marked `CONCENTRATOR-ONLY`. Required keys are shown live; optional/defaulted
598	keys are shown **commented-out with their default value**. Uncommenting a key
599	and leaving it at the shown value is a no-op. The per-section notes after the
600	example capture the cross-field constraints a single example cannot express
601	inline. All of these are enforced at config load (`config.Load`), which fails
602	fast on the first violation — except the loopback and `allowed_ips` checks,
603	noted below.
604	
605	> The same content ships as a copy-pasteable file at the repo root,
606	> [`wanbond.example.toml`](../wanbond.example.toml): copy it, fill the
607	> `<placeholders>`, and delete what you do not need.
608	
609	```toml
610	# ── top level ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
611	role = "edge"                      # REQUIRED. "edge" | "concentrator". Never
612	                                   #   inferred from other keys.
613	psk  = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"      # REQUIRED. 32 raw bytes, base64. Same value
614	                                   #   on both ends; keys the PSK-HMAC that
615	                                   #   authenticates outer PROBE/CONTROL frames.
616	# tun_persist = false              # OPTIONAL, DEFAULT false. false => wanbond0 is
617	                                   #   destroyed on daemon stop (its addresses/
618	                                   #   routes/rules drop on every restart). true =>
619	                                   #   TUNSETPERSIST on start: wanbond0 survives a
620	                                   #   restart with the SAME ifindex, so operator-
